did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes I3DermoscopyApp, which uses image processing algorithms and Machine Learning techniques for the automatic detection of melanoma. Specifically, it employs techniques such as Laplacian of Gaussian (LOG) filtering, Frangi’s Filter for vessel enhancement, and mentions the adoption of Machine Learning for classification and the potential use of Decision Trees to train and test a classifier for the Seven Point Checklist diagnostic method.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents I3DermoscopyApp, a novel web-based application for melanoma diagnosis, and reports original experimental validation results. It details the system architecture, image processing algorithms (e.g., Seven Point Checklist implementation), and provides quantitative performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, diagnostic accuracy) from a study involving dermatologists and a dermoscopic database, published in the Proceedings of the 50th Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences.

title: I3DermoscopyApp: Hacking Melanoma thanks to IoT technologies
journal: Proceedings of the 50th Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences
brief_summary: This study introduces I3DermoscopyApp, a web-based application leveraging IoT technologies to support the early detection of melanoma through the automatic application of the Seven Point Checklist diagnostic method. The system utilizes image processing algorithms, including Laplacian of Gaussian filtering and Frangi’s Filter, to extract morphological and chromatic parameters from dermoscopic images captured via smartphones. The platform enables physicians to store images, measure lesion features, and receive an automated second opinion to assist in biopsy decision-making. Validation was performed on a dermoscopic database by comparing the system's automated classification against expert dermatologists' diagnoses using sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic accuracy metrics. The results demonstrated that while the system did not outperform expert dermatologists, it significantly improved the diagnostic sensitivity and accuracy of non-expert dermatologists, highlighting its potential as a clinical support tool for less experienced clinicians.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant melanoma by the system and biopsy reference; 64 cases in the database.
Classified as atypical nevus by the system and biopsy reference; 99 cases in the database (including suspicious nevi).
Sub-class of atypical nevi; 22 cases in the database; classified as 'suspicious nevus to be followed-up' by the system.
Classified as benign nevus by the system and biopsy reference; 107 cases in the database.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of the skin lesion (malignant melanoma, atypical nevus, suspicious nevus, or benign nevus) based on the Seven Point Checklist scoring system.
all_task_types.details: The system performs automatic classification of pigmented skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories (malignant melanoma, atypical nevus, suspicious nevus, benign nevus) using a weighted scoring version of the Seven Point Checklist. The classification is determined by summing weighted scores for detected dermoscopic criteria and comparing the total score against thresholds (e.g., TS,w < 1.5 for Benign nevus, TS,w > 3.0 for Melanoma). The paper explicitly states the lesion is 'classified as' one of these categories.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopic Database from University "Federico II" of Naples
datasets_used.description: A collection of 270 Epiluminescence Microscopy (ELM) images collected during a screening campaign at the Dept. of Systematic Pathology - Section of Dermatology at University "Federico II" of Naples, Italy. Images were captured using iPhone 4/5 smartphones equipped with DermLite DL1 or Handyscope dermoscopes. The database includes corresponding dermoscopic and clinical information (patient age, sex, lesion position, first opinion, histological results).

use.validation: The database was used to evaluate the diagnostic and clinical accuracy of the automatic measurement system (MS) and two groups of dermatologists (Expert Dermatologists ED and Non-Expert Dermatologists NED) by comparing their Seven Point Checklist scores against biopsy reference results.
use.testing: The database served as the test set for validating the I3DermoscopyApp's automatic classification and measurement performance against expert dermatologists and biopsy ground truth.
use.other: The database was also used to assess the improvement in diagnostic sensitivity and accuracy for non-expert dermatologists when assisted by the system (NED + MS).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the casual distribution of linear/globular objects and determine the detection reliability of Atypical Vascular Pattern by testing the Null Hypothesis regarding the spatial distribution of structures in lesion quadrants.
To compute diagnostic accuracy as a weighted mean of sensitivity and specificity, accounting for the numerosity of different lesion classes.

To characterize the statistical validity of the results and exclude the possibility that the variation in diagnostic accuracy between non-expert dermatologists with and without system assistance (NED vs NED + MS) was purely casual.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The spatial distribution of linear/globular structures (Atypical Vascular Pattern) within the lesion quadrants is uniform (random/casual).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The spatial distribution of linear/globular structures is not uniform (indicating an Atypical Vascular Pattern).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Binomial Test was used to estimate the casual distribution of linear/globular objects to determine detection reliability; if the paucity or plenty of objects in any quadrant/couple of quadrants was observed, the Null Hypothesis was refused. Additionally, Cohen's Kappa test was used to show statistical validity of results, excluding the possibility that the variation in diagnostic accuracy between non-expert dermatologists with and without system assistance was purely casual.

Dermoscopic Database from University \"F.dataset_description: 270 Epiluminescence Microscopy (ELM) images: 64 Malignant Melanoma (MN), 99 Atypical Nevi (AN, including 22 Suspicious Nevi), 107 Benign Nevi (BN).
Dermoscopic Database from University \"F.evaluation_setting: Comparison of diagnostic performance between Expert Dermatologists (ED), Non-Expert Dermatologists (NED), and the Automatic Measurement System (MS). A second evaluation compared NED alone vs NED assisted by MS (NED + MS).

Biopsy Reference.Note: Ground truth for MN (64 cases), AN (73 cases), SN (26 cases), BN (107 cases).

Dermoscopic Database from University \"F.notes: The ED group showed the best performance (Sensitivity ~80-92%, Specificity ~90-95%). The MS showed good sensitivity (86%) but low specificity (74%). NED physicians significantly benefited from MS assistance, with diagnostic sensitivity improving by 14-17% and diagnostic accuracy by 2-4%, without compromising specificity. Cohen's Kappa test confirmed the statistical validity of the improvement in NED performance with MS assistance.

architecture_details.framework: Distributed application consisting of a mobile app front-end and a web-based platform.
components: Application Engine (Automatic Measurement and Diagnosis System)

feature_extraction: Multi-Thresholding based on Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
classification_methods: Weighted version of the Seven Point Checklist scoring system

architecture_details.implementation: Matlab routines executed as distributed code (jobs with multiple parallel tasks) using Parallel Toolbox and web communication with Apache server.

training_details.dataset: Digital ELM image collection of over 600 skin lesions (partly from reference atlas, partly from Dept. of Dermatology of University Federico II, Naples, 2008-2013)
training_details.method: Supervised learning for classification algorithms; Statistical analysis for reliability of criterion detection.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Diagnostic category based on Seven Point Checklist
classification_or_output_head.scoring_mechanism: Weighted version of the Seven Point Checklist scoring system

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Weighted version of the Seven Point Checklist scoring system
preprocessing_techniques: Statistical Region Merging (SRM) for boundary detection
Multi-Thresholding based on Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
Laplacian of Gaussian (LOG) filtering for feature extraction
conclusions: The I3DermoscopyApp, a web-based application leveraging IoT technologies and image processing algorithms (including Laplacian of Gaussian filtering and Frangi’s Filter), was developed to support the automatic application of the Seven Point Checklist diagnostic method for melanoma detection.

The automatic measurement system (MS) achieved a sensitivity of 86% but a lower specificity of 74%, resulting in a diagnostic accuracy of 77%, which did not outperform the performance of expert dermatologists (who achieved sensitivities of 84-92% and specificities of 94-95%).

The system significantly improved the diagnostic performance of non-expert dermatologists (NED); when assisted by the MS, their diagnostic sensitivity increased by 14-17% (from 66-72% to 83-86%) and diagnostic accuracy increased by 2-4% (from 85-86% to 88-89%), without compromising specificity.
Statistical validity analysis (Cohen's Kappa test) confirmed that the improvement in diagnostic accuracy for non-expert dermatologists using the system was not due to chance.

The study concludes that while artificial dermoscopy tools may not outperform expert dermatologists, they are highly useful as clinical support tools to provide second opinions and improve the diagnostic performance of physicians with short clinical experience.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset of 270 images collected from University "Federico II" of Naples, lacking external validation on diverse populations or devices.
The Automatic Measurement System (MS) demonstrated low specificity (74%), primarily due to the slight over-estimation of both major and minor dermoscopic criteria compared to expert baselines.
The system did not outperform expert dermatologists (who achieved 84-92% sensitivity and 94-95% specificity), indicating it is not a standalone diagnostic tool but rather a support aid.

The primary clinical value identified is the significant improvement in diagnostic sensitivity (14-17%) and accuracy (2-4%) for non-expert dermatologists with less than 3 years of experience when assisted by the system.
Data privacy and security concerns are noted, with sensitive patient records stored in the cloud managed by the platform owner for 5 years, relying on HTTPS encryption as the minimum security level.

The dataset includes a small number of 'Suspicious Nevi' (22 cases) grouped within 'Atypical Nevi' (99 cases), which may limit the granularity of classification performance analysis for this specific sub-class.
The image acquisition hardware was limited to iPhone 4/5 smartphones equipped with DermLite DL1 or Handyscope dermoscopes, potentially limiting generalizability to other imaging devices.

The study relies on a weighted scoring system based on detection reliability (Low, Medium, High) rather than purely deep learning, with machine learning techniques (Decision Trees, etc.) mentioned only as future work.
